---
title: "First-order syntax and substitution"
---

::: {#def-fol-signature .definition name="First-order signature" date="2026-07-12"}
A first-order signature \(L\) consists of sets \(\mathcal F_n\) of \(n\)-ary
function symbols and sets \(\mathcal R_n\) of \(n\)-ary relation symbols for
each \(n\in\mathbb N\).  Constants are the members of \(\mathcal F_0\).
Equality is a distinguished logical binary predicate, not a member of
\(\mathcal R_2\).  A language extension keeps the old symbols with their
arities and adds new ones.
:::

::: {#def-fol-terms .definition name="Terms and variables" date="2026-07-12"}
Fix a countably infinite set \(\mathrm{Var}\) of variables.  For a signature
\(L\) as in @def-fol-signature, the \(L\)-terms are the least set containing
every variable and every constant and closed under
\(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) for \(f\in\mathcal F_n\).  A term is *closed* when it
has no variable.  The finite set of variables occurring in \(t\) is
\(\mathrm{Var}(t)\).
:::

::: {#def-fol-formulas .definition name="Formulas and sentences" date="2026-07-12"}
For @def-fol-signature and @def-fol-terms, atomic formulas are
\(R(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) and \(t=u\).  Formulas are generated from atomic
formulas using \(\neg\), \(\wedge\), and \(\exists x\).  Write the other
Boolean connectives and \(\forall x\,A:=\neg\exists x\neg A\) as
abbreviations.  Every variable occurrence in a term is free.  In an atomic
formula all occurrences are free; negation preserves free occurrences,
conjunction takes their union, and \(\exists x A\) binds exactly the free
occurrences of \(x\) in \(A\).  Thus
\(\mathrm{FV}(\exists x A)=\mathrm{FV}(A)\setminus\{x\}\), with the evident
recursive clauses for atomic formulas, negation, and conjunction.  A sentence
has no free variables.  Alpha-equivalence is the least congruence identifying
\(\exists x A\) with \(\exists y\,A[y/x]\) whenever \(y\) is fresh for
\(A\); formulas are considered modulo this congruence.
:::

::: {#def-fol-substitution .definition name="Capture-avoiding substitution" date="2026-07-12"}
For a term \(t\), variable \(x\), and formula \(A\) from @def-fol-formulas,
\(A[t/x]\) is simultaneous replacement of the free occurrences of \(x\) by
\(t\), after renaming bound variables when necessary so that no variable of
\(t\) becomes bound.  The notation
\(A[t_1/x_1,\ldots,t_k/x_k]\) means simultaneous capture-avoiding
substitution.  A term \(t\) is *free for* \(x\) in \(A\) when the direct
replacement causes no capture.
:::

::: {#lem-fresh-variable .lemma name="Fresh-variable lemma" date="2026-07-12"}
For every finite collection of terms and formulas there is a variable that
does not occur in any member of the collection.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-fresh-variable"}
Every term and formula has a finite parse tree by @def-fol-terms and
@def-fol-formulas, hence contains finitely many variables.  A finite union of
finite sets is finite, while \(\mathrm{Var}\) in @def-fol-terms is infinite.
Choose a variable outside that union.
:::

::: {#lem-substitution-composition .lemma name="Substitution composition" date="2026-07-12"}
Let \(A\) be a formula, let \(x\ne y\), and suppose the displayed
substitutions are capture-free and \(y\notin\mathrm{Var}(t)\).  Then
\[
 (A[u/y])[t/x]= (A[t/x])[u[t/x]/y]
\]
up to alpha-equivalence.  The analogous identity holds for terms.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-substitution-composition"}
Use @lem-fresh-variable to rename every binder of \(A\) away from all
variables occurring in \(t,u\).  For variables and constants the term identity
follows directly from substitution; the function-term case follows by applying
the induction hypothesis to every argument.  For formulas, atomic cases follow
from the term identity, while negation and conjunction follow by applying the
induction hypothesis to their immediate subformulas.
For \(\exists z B\), the chosen renaming makes \(z\) distinct from
\(x,y\) and absent from \(t,u\), so both sides are obtained by applying the
induction hypothesis inside the unchanged binder \(\exists z\).  This proves
the identity specified by @def-fol-substitution.
:::

::: {#def-language-translation .definition name="Reduct translation" date="2026-07-12"}
If \(L\subseteq L'\) and \(A\) is an \(L'\)-formula, replacing selected new
constants by distinct variables means the capture-avoiding simultaneous
substitution of those variables for the constants, after bound-variable
renaming as in @def-fol-substitution.  It fixes every \(L\)-symbol and
commutes with the logical constructors of @def-fol-formulas.
:::
